past_medical_history: Bipolar disorder type II, diagnosed 20 years ago, stable on valproic acid
Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, controlled on amlodipine
Chronic constipation, worsening over the past 3 years
REM sleep disturbance reported by wife for approximately 2 years
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 5 years ago
Episode of prolonged febrile illness with altered sensorium 18 months ago, treated at outside hospital with broad-spectrum antibiotics; discharged with diagnosis of encephalitis of uncertain etiology
family_history: Mother had essential tremor beginning in her 60s
Father died of complications of dementia at age 82, type unspecified
No known family history of Parkinson's disease

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 beers per week; abstinent during mood episodes
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 3 miles daily; has reduced to short walks around the block due to unsteadiness over the past year
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in single-story home; independent in most ADLs but wife reports increasing slowness and need for assistance with buttons and zippers

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score 24/30, losing points on trail-making (1), delayed recall (2), verbal fluency (2), and abstraction (1). Speech is hypophonic with mild monotone quality but content is fluent and coherent. No aphasia, neglect, or ideomotor apraxia. Affect somewhat flat but patient reports his mood is stable.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal round and reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact; no nystagmus. No oculogyric phenomena elicited during examination. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. Marked facial hypomimia with reduced blink rate and decreased emotional expression. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Moderate cogwheel rigidity at bilateral wrists, left greater than right. Mild axial rigidity noted on passive neck flexion. Bradykinesia evident on finger tapping, hand opening-closing, and pronation-supination, with progressive decrement in amplitude and speed, more prominent on the left. Mild resting tremor of the left hand at 4-5 Hz, suppressed with action. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Vibration sense mildly reduced at bilateral ankles. Romberg test negative.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact. Rapid alternating movements slowed bilaterally, left more than right, consistent with bradykinesia rather than cerebellar dysfunction.
neurological_exam.gait: Narrow-based gait with reduced stride length and decreased bilateral arm swing, left arm swing nearly absent. Mild forward-stooped posture. Mild festination noted on turns. Two to three corrective steps on pull test indicating mildly impaired postural reflexes. No freezing episodes observed. Able to rise from chair without arm assistance but slowly.
neurological_exam.additional: Glabellar reflex positive (Myerson sign — fails to habituate). Mild seborrheic dermatitis on scalp and nasolabial folds. Reduced olfaction reported by patient but not formally tested. No myoclonus. No dystonia.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive slowness and stiffness with a new tremor in the left hand over the past 10 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Gerald Novak is a 68-year-old right-handed Caucasian man with a longstanding history of bipolar disorder type II who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic for evaluation of progressive motor slowing, stiffness, and a new tremor that have developed over the past 10 months. His wife, who accompanies him, first noticed that his facial expressions had become less animated and his voice softer approximately a year ago. Over the subsequent months she observed that he was walking more slowly with shorter steps and had begun to shuffle, particularly when turning. About 6 months ago he developed an intermittent tremor of his left hand that is most noticeable when the hand is at rest, such as when sitting watching television, and diminishes when he reaches for objects. He has also experienced progressive difficulty with fine motor tasks including buttoning his shirt, manipulating a fork, and his handwriting has become noticeably smaller. The patient attributes some of his symptoms to aging and residual effects from a significant illness 18 months ago. At that time he was hospitalized for approximately two weeks at an outside facility with high fevers, altered mental status, intermittent rigidity, and prolonged somnolence. He was treated empirically with broad-spectrum antibiotics and eventually improved, though he and his wife feel he never fully returned to his prior baseline. The diagnosis at discharge was listed as encephalitis of uncertain etiology. His psychiatrist had discontinued olanzapine approximately 8 weeks before that hospitalization due to metabolic concerns, and his current psychiatric regimen of valproic acid and low-dose clonazepam has been stable for over a year. His wife also reports that for the past two years he has been increasingly active during sleep, talking loudly, flailing his arms, and on at least two occasions falling out of bed. His constipation has worsened progressively over the past three years despite daily polyethylene glycol. He denies visual hallucinations, urinary incontinence, or significant cognitive complaints, though his wife notes he is slower to respond in conversations and occasionally loses his train of thought. He denies any current use of antipsychotic medications or dopamine-blocking antiemetics.

findings.location: Scattered periventricular and deep white matter, bilateral

additional_observations: No restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemic event
No basal ganglia signal abnormality or calcification
No mass lesion or midline shift
Sulcal prominence proportional to ventricular size, not suggestive of NPH
Midbrain and pontine contour normal; no hummingbird sign, no hot cross bun sign
Putaminal volume and signal intensity normal bilaterally
Nonspecific periventricular T2 hyperintensities likely represent chronic small vessel ischemic changes
mri.impression: No structural lesion, acute infarction, or abnormal signal in the basal ganglia to account for parkinsonian symptoms. No imaging features of MSA (no pontine or cerebellar atrophy, no hot cross bun sign) or PSP (no midbrain atrophy, no hummingbird sign). Normal-appearing substantia nigra on susceptibility-weighted sequences. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral posterior putamina, asymmetric, left putamen more affected

additional_observations: The degree of DAT reduction exceeds what would be expected from valproic acid–induced parkinsonism, which typically shows normal DaTscan
Pattern is also inconsistent with purely postencephalitic parkinsonism in which DaTscan may be normal or show variable patterns
output.impression: Abnormal DaTscan demonstrating asymmetric bilateral reduction in dopamine transporter uptake in the posterior putamina, markedly worse on the left. The findings argue against valproic acid–induced parkinsonism or postencephalitic parkinsonism as the sole explanation, as these conditions typically show normal or nonspecific DaTscan patterns. Clinical correlation recommended.

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.test: Post-levodopa UPDRS-III Motor Score (250 mg carbidopa/levodopa)

Polysomnography.value: Yes — three episodes of vigorous arm movements, vocalizations, and one episode of sitting up abruptly during REM

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Total Sleep Time 298 minutes (L), Sleep Efficiency 68% % (H), REM without Atonia (%) 48% % of REM epochs (H), PLMI (Periodic Limb Movement Index) 22 events/hour (H), Dream enactment behaviors observed Yes — three episodes of vigorous arm movements, vocalizations, and one episode of sitting up abruptly during REM  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: REM without Atonia: 48% of REM epochs (H)
Dream enactment behaviors: Present — three episodes confirmed on video-PSG (H)

Autonomic_Reflex_Screen.test: Head-up tilt table test — BP drop
Autonomic_Reflex_Screen.value: Systolic drop of 15 mmHg at 3 minutes
Autonomic_Reflex_Screen.reference_range: Orthostatic hypotension defined as >20 mmHg systolic drop

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test — Delayed Recall

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: MoCA Total Score 24 /30 (L), Trail Making Test A — Time 48 seconds (H), Trail Making Test B — Time 118 seconds (H), Phonemic verbal fluency (FAS) 28 words/3 min (L), Category fluency (Animals) 13 words/min (L).

output.query: Postencephalitic parkinsonism versus idiopathic Parkinson's disease differential diagnosis

results.title: Encephalitis Lethargica and Postencephalitic Parkinsonism: A Modern Perspective
results.authors: Casals J, Elizan TS, Yahr MD
results.key_finding: Postencephalitic parkinsonism (PEP) differs from idiopathic PD in several key features: PEP typically presents acutely following an encephalitic illness, may feature oculogyric crises, and often shows dramatic response to levodopa. Unlike PD, PEP is not progressive after the initial encephalitic event and DaTscan may be normal or show variable patterns distinct from the typical asymmetric posterior putaminal loss of PD.

results.title: DaTscan Findings in Secondary Parkinsonism: A Systematic Review
results.key_finding: In drug-induced and postencephalitic parkinsonism, DaTscan is typically normal or shows nonspecific mild bilateral reduction. In contrast, idiopathic PD shows characteristic asymmetric putaminal reduction with caudate sparing. An abnormal DaTscan in a patient with parkinsonism after encephalitis should raise concern for coincident idiopathic PD or PD unmasked by the encephalitic event.

results.title: Valproic Acid–Induced Parkinsonism: Clinical Features and Outcomes
results.key_finding: Valproic acid–induced parkinsonism typically presents with symmetric features, postural tremor, and is generally reversible upon withdrawal of valproate within weeks to months. DaTscan is normal in pure valproate-induced parkinsonism. Persistence of symptoms beyond 6 months after drug withdrawal suggests an alternative or co-existing etiology.

output.summary: The differential between postencephalitic parkinsonism, valproate-induced parkinsonism, and idiopathic PD is best resolved by DaTscan and clinical follow-up. DaTscan showing asymmetric posterior putaminal reduction strongly favors idiopathic PD. Postencephalitic parkinsonism is typically nonprogressive after the acute event, while PD is slowly progressive. Valproate-induced parkinsonism resolves with drug withdrawal and shows normal DaTscan. The presence of premotor features (RBD, constipation, hyposmia) predating any confounding exposures further supports underlying idiopathic PD.

output.proposed: Valproic acid 1000 mg nightly (current medication) — interaction check for parkinsonian symptoms
interactions: Valproic acid has been associated with reversible parkinsonism including tremor, rigidity, and gait disturbance in 5-10% of patients on chronic therapy
Mechanism may involve GABAergic effects on basal ganglia circuits or direct neurotoxicity
Concomitant use of valproic acid with levodopa/carbidopa is not pharmacologically antagonistic (unlike dopamine blockers), but valproate-induced tremor may confound assessment of levodopa response
contraindications: Valproic acid is not absolutely contraindicated in PD but should be used with caution
If parkinsonism is suspected to be valproate-induced, a trial withdrawal should be considered if psychiatrically safe
warnings: Valproic acid can cause hyperammonemia even with normal liver function, potentially contributing to encephalopathic features
Chronic valproate use can cause tremor that mimics or exacerbates parkinsonian tremor
Withdrawal of valproate must be gradual and coordinated with psychiatry to avoid mood destabilization or seizures
alternatives: Lithium carbonate (mood stabilizer; may cause tremor but by different mechanism)
Lamotrigine (mood stabilizer with lower tremor risk; good option for bipolar maintenance)
Carbamazepine (mood stabilizer; generally lower parkinsonian risk but enzyme-inducing)

output.query: Valproic acid induced parkinsonism clinical features DaTscan reversibility

results.title: Valproate-Induced Parkinsonism: A Review of the Literature
results.key_finding: Valproate-induced parkinsonism occurs in approximately 5-10% of patients on chronic therapy, typically within months to years of initiation. Features include symmetric parkinsonian signs (most commonly postural tremor and rigidity), gait disturbance, and cognitive slowing. Symptoms are generally reversible within 1-3 months of drug withdrawal. DaTscan is normal in pure valproate-induced parkinsonism.

results.title: Distinguishing Drug-Induced from Neurodegenerative Parkinsonism: The Role of DaTscan
results.key_finding: In a cohort of patients with suspected drug-induced parkinsonism, approximately 15-20% had abnormal DaTscans, indicating underlying preclinical neurodegenerative disease unmasked by the offending agent. Asymmetric DaTscan reduction and presence of non-motor prodromal features (RBD, hyposmia, constipation) were the strongest predictors of underlying PD.

output.summary: Valproate-induced parkinsonism is a well-recognized complication of chronic valproate therapy, typically presenting with symmetric features and resolving after drug withdrawal. DaTscan is the key differentiator: normal in purely drug-induced parkinsonism, abnormal in neurodegenerative PD. In approximately 15-20% of patients with suspected drug-induced parkinsonism, DaTscan reveals underlying preclinical PD that was unmasked by the drug.

differential.key_features: Prior febrile illness with altered sensorium and rigidity 18 months ago, with parkinsonian features emerging afterward. However, progressive worsening, asymmetric DaTscan reduction, and RBD predating the encephalitic episode favor underlying PD

Patient on chronic valproic acid 1000 mg nightly with known association with reversible parkinsonism. However, DaTscan is abnormal (would be normal in pure drug-induced parkinsonism), premotor features predate valproate-related symptom timeline, and asymmetry favors PD

Constipation and mild autonomic features could raise concern. However, autonomic testing shows only borderline dysfunction, MRI shows no pontine or cerebellar atrophy, and robust levodopa response argues against MSA
Mild axial rigidity and postural instability could suggest PSP. However, no vertical gaze palsy, good levodopa response, asymmetric limb involvement, and no midbrain atrophy on MRI argue against it

RBD, mild cognitive impairment, and parkinsonism are shared features. However, motor symptoms clearly preceded cognitive changes by at least a year (the 1-year rule), no visual hallucinations or fluctuating cognition, favoring PD over DLB

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including timeline of symptom onset, prior encephalitic illness, medication history (especially olanzapine discontinuation and valproic acid duration), and premotor features (sleep disturbance, constipation, anosmia)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Premotor symptoms (RBD, constipation) predated the encephalitic illness and valproic acid. Progressive motor decline over 10 months since encephalitic episode. Olanzapine discontinued 8 weeks before the hospitalization.

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination focusing on asymmetry, tremor characteristics, eye movements, and assessment for oculogyric crises
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Asymmetric parkinsonism (left > right) with resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, facial hypomimia, and mildly impaired postural reflexes. No oculogyric crises or vertical gaze palsy.

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to evaluate for structural causes of parkinsonism and to assess for features of atypical parkinsonian syndromes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal MRI with mild age-appropriate changes and no structural cause for parkinsonism; no features of MSA or PSP

optimal_actions.action: Order routine laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, B12, RPR, liver function, ammonia level, and valproic acid level
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal results except mildly elevated ammonia (valproic acid effect). No metabolic cause for parkinsonism identified.

optimal_actions.action: Order DaTscan to differentiate neurodegenerative PD from secondary parkinsonism (postencephalitic or valproate-induced)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Abnormal DaTscan showing asymmetric bilateral reduction in putaminal DAT uptake, confirming underlying nigrostriatal degeneration consistent with idiopathic PD

optimal_actions.action: Evaluate valproic acid contribution and discuss with psychiatry about trial withdrawal or substitution with alternative mood stabilizer
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Valproate can cause/exacerbate parkinsonism; trial withdrawal should be considered in coordination with psychiatry

optimal_actions.action: Order polysomnography to evaluate reported dream enactment behaviors for REM sleep behavior disorder
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed RBD, a synucleinopathy biomarker supporting PD diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Consider levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness and guide treatment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive response (42% UPDRS improvement) supporting idiopathic PD diagnosis and indicating likely benefit from dopaminergic therapy

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dopaminergic therapy (carbidopa/levodopa) at low dose with gradual titration
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical improvement in motor symptoms with levodopa therapy

optimal_actions.action: Refer for multidisciplinary rehabilitation: physical therapy for gait and balance, occupational therapy for fine motor function, speech therapy for hypophonia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improved functional status with multidisciplinary approach

critical_actions: Recognize that prior encephalitic illness and valproic acid use are confounders that could explain some parkinsonian features but do not fully account for the clinical picture
Order DaTscan to definitively differentiate neurodegenerative PD from secondary parkinsonism
Identify premotor features (RBD, constipation, hyposmia) as evidence of underlying synucleinopathy predating confounding exposures
Evaluate valproic acid as a potential contributor to parkinsonism and coordinate with psychiatry regarding possible substitution
Initiate levodopa therapy for a patient with confirmed nigrostriatal degeneration and positive levodopa response
contraindicated_actions: Attributing all parkinsonian symptoms to the prior encephalitic illness without further workup
Attributing all symptoms to valproic acid without DaTscan confirmation
Starting antipsychotic medications (typical or atypical except quetiapine/clozapine) for psychiatric management
Abruptly discontinuing valproic acid without psychiatric coordination, risking mood destabilization
Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., trihexyphenidyl) in a 68-year-old with mild cognitive impairment
Resuming olanzapine or other dopamine-blocking antipsychotics
key_reasoning_points: The prior encephalitic illness with rigidity and altered sensorium creates a strong confound: postencephalitic parkinsonism (encephalitis lethargica) is a recognized cause of secondary parkinsonism, and the temporal relationship is suggestive
However, multiple premotor PD features (RBD for 2 years, progressive constipation for 3 years) predated the encephalitic episode, suggesting underlying PD was already developing before that illness
Chronic valproic acid use is a second confound, as valproate-induced parkinsonism occurs in 5-10% of patients and is generally reversible upon withdrawal

DaTscan is the critical differentiator: normal in drug-induced and typically normal/nonspecific in postencephalitic parkinsonism, but shows characteristic asymmetric putaminal reduction in idiopathic PD

The asymmetric presentation (left hand tremor, left-predominant rigidity) with rest tremor is a classic PD pattern and would be atypical for either valproate-induced or postencephalitic parkinsonism, which tend to be more symmetric
RBD is pathognomonic of synucleinopathy and would not be caused by valproic acid, clonazepam, or a prior encephalitic illness — its presence strongly supports underlying PD or DLB

The mother's essential tremor in the family history is a red herring — essential tremor and PD are distinct conditions, though family history of ET has been epidemiologically associated with slightly increased PD risk
Father's unspecified dementia could raise concern for a hereditary neurodegenerative condition but is nonspecific and does not change the diagnostic approach
The mildly elevated ammonia from valproic acid could theoretically contribute to cognitive slowing but does not explain the progressive asymmetric motor syndrome

metadata.difficulty_description: Multiple confounders complicate the diagnosis: a prior encephalitic illness suggesting postencephalitic parkinsonism, chronic valproic acid use which can cause reversible parkinsonism, and a family history of essential tremor. The agent must recognize that premotor features predated these exposures and use DaTscan to confirm underlying neurodegenerative disease.
